text: We present the surgical technique for robotic total thyroidectomy with central neck dissection by bilateral axillary breast approach
The anterior border of the right sternocleidomastoid muscle and the neck midline are visually outlined, providing clear anatomical landmarks for the procedure.
The surgical robot is docked, and the camera along with endurance instruments are introduced into the surgical field, following the raising of the flap.
BABA is an endoscopic remote access to the neck that can be performed either with conventional endoscopic or robotic technology.
In this case, the DaVinci SI is used.

The surgeon introduces a thirty-degree endoscope through the right axillary port, providing a downward view over the surgical field. The endoscope's position offers a perspective similar to that of a conventional open thyroidectomy, with anatomical visualization from a slightly lateral, inferior, and anterior angle.
In this video we will focus on the surgical technique as it slightly differs from the conventional thyroidectomy.
The surgeon manipulates three robotic arms alongside the camera arm within the surgical field, utilizing the DaVinci SI system for precise control during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses the PK Dissector, a holding and dissection tool with bipolar energy, in the left hand through the right axillary port to manipulate tissue within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a cutting instrument, alternating between a monopolar hook and a harmonic device, with the right hand coming through the left breast port. The camera is positioned between the surgeon's hands, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a bipolar fenestrated forceps, known as the prograsp, inserted through the left axillary port via the fourth robotic arm, to manipulate tissue within the surgical field.
The secondary instrument is positioned in the surgeon's right hand to assist in exposing the surgical field, complementing the primary instruments used for dissection.
The surgeon reaches the lateral border of the lobe and begins searching for the recurrent laryngeal nerve, using robotic arms for precise manipulation.
The surgeon uses the monopolar hook as a probe, maneuvering it within the surgical field to explore and manipulate tissue during the thymectomy procedure.
The patient is under intraoperative nerve monitoring with surface electrodes in the endotracheal tube.
The surgeon connects the cable of the nerve stimulator to the monopolar hook, replacing the energy cable, allowing the hook to function as a probe for nerve monitoring.
The surgeon uses the monopolar hook to map the recurrent laryngeal nerve, navigating between anatomical landmarks visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon follows the thyroid capsule to carefully free the gland from its attachments, ensuring protection of the parathyroid glands and sealing vascular structures to prevent bleeding.
The surgeon detaches the lower pole of the thyroid from the trachea while simultaneously dissecting the left central compartment, carefully maneuvering around the anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects the nerve closer to the trachea and the ligament of Bary, using a soaked cotinoid to shield the nerve from potential thermal injury during the procedure.
The surgeon further dissects the thyroid lobe from the trachea, leaving the superior neurovascular pedicle intact.
The surgeon frees the superior thyroid pole from the trachea, carefully detaching it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon detaches the thyroid lobe from the trachea, leaving it attached only to the superior neurovascular pedicle.
The surgeon uses a probe to identify the superior laryngeal nerve, while the superior thyroid vein and artery are selectively ligated using a harmonic scalpel.
The surgical specimen is placed over the right external carotid muscle (ECM) for temporary storage, awaiting removal from the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a cotinoid to protect the right recurrent laryngeal nerve during dissection in the central compartment, ensuring the nerve is shielded from potential damage.
The surgical specimen is placed over the right sternocleidomastoid muscle (ECM) for temporary storage.
The surgeon shifts focus to the left lobe of the thyroid, preparing to continue the dissection.
Same steps are followed.
The surgeon applies medial traction to the thyroid isthmus and continues the dissection of the thyroid lobe, carefully manipulating the tissue.
The surgeon identifies the recurrent laryngeal nerve, ensuring its visibility and preservation during the dissection in the central compartment.
The surgeon identifies the superior laryngeal nerve and ligates the vascular superior pedicle, securing the blood vessels in the surgical field.
The surgeon irrigates the surgical field with saline, checks for hemostasis on both sides, and applies hemostatic glue along with fibrillar Surgicel to control bleeding.
The surgeon sutures the strap muscles in the midline using a barred V-lock suture, securing the muscles together with visible precision.
A surgical drain is placed through the left axillary trochlear incision, completing the procedure.
Total console time was exactly one hour.
The surgical procedure leaves no visible scars.
